question_text: Two identical light bulbs are connected to a 12 V power supply. In circuit P, both bulbs are connected in series. In circuit Q, both bulbs are connected in parallel. What is the potential difference across each individual bulb in each circuit?

options.label: A
options.text: Each bulb receives 12 V in both circuits

options.label: B
options.text: Each bulb receives 6 V in series and 12 V in parallel

options.label: C
options.text: Each bulb receives 12 V in series and 6 V in parallel

options.label: D
options.text: The potential difference is the same regardless of circuit arrangement

correct_answer: Each bulb receives 6 V in series and 12 V in parallel
explanation: In a series circuit, the power supply's voltage is divided equally between all components. With two identical bulbs and a 12 V supply, each bulb gets 6 V. In a parallel circuit, each branch receives the full voltage from the power supply, so each bulb gets the full 12 V. This is why bulbs are brighter in parallel—they receive more potential difference and therefore more power.
